Abstract
--------

Rapid urbanization and industrialization with their negative repercussions draw attention to the need for urban sustainability. This research built on the urban systems understanding linked with resource utilization, environmental effects, people-environment interactions, and adaptive management by integrating quantitative modelling of urban metabolism with a qualitative investigation into human responses. A hybrid methodology was applied in a typical industrial city of Jinchang, China, which exemplified a model of how complex urban dynamics link to and inform sustainability transitions. The study found that Jinchang City had taken on an unsustainable trajectory underpinned by continuous growth of material consumption, whereas a potential for sustainability transitions was also observed in decoupled negative material outputs, improved material efficiency, and environment regeneration collectively. The challenges in industrial cities have been framed in terms of development model lock-in, conceptual and institutional constraints, innovation and human capital deficiencies, and governance failure. The research proposed a government-led transformation system that integrates multi-level and multi-perspective transformations and management employing discipline insights to facilitate urban sustainability transitions. This involves governance, economic and technological innovations, social transformations, and implementations, in which urban governance and public engagement are essential elements for adapting to undesirable difficulties and exploring locally appropriate strategies. This research provided a theoretical and empirical basis along with scientific and policy implications, which contributed to the developing interdisciplinary knowledge on the understanding of complex urban systems and provided insights into real-world sustainability solutions.

Introduction
------------

Economic expansion, intensive resource usage, energy consumption, waste creation, environmental contamination, and accompanying social issues all occur in tandem with urbanization. Although urbanization is not the unique and direct cause to environmental issues, it will continue to have a profound effect on the natural environment (Bettencourt and West, [2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR8 "Bettencourt L, West G (2010) A unified theory of urban living. Nature 467(7318):912–913"); Mumford, [1961](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR48 "Mumford L (1961) The city in history: its origins, its transformations, and its prospects. Harcourt, Brace & World, New York")), while ecological problems, social conflicts, health problems, and a slew of other negative consequences will occur if urbanization is not treated effectively (Asabere et al. [2020](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR2 "Asabere SB, Acheampong RA, Ashiagbor G et al. (2020) Urbanization, land use transformation and spatio-environmental impacts: Analyses of trends and implications in major metropolitan regions of Ghana. Land Use Policy 96:104707"); Bateman and Hochman, [1971](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR4 "Bateman W, Hochman HM (1971) Social problems and the urban crisis: can public policy make a difference? Am Econ Rev 61(2):346–353")). Nonetheless, urbanization contributes to environmental conservation and social-economic prosperity by improved resource use efficiency and environmental management (Fan and Qi, [2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR23 "Fan P, Qi J (2010) Assessing the sustainability of major cities in China. Sustain Sci 5(1):51–68"); Hui et al. [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR35 "Hui CX, Dan G, Alamri S et al. (2023) Greening smart cities: An investigation of the integration of urban natural resources and smart city technologies for promoting environmental sustainability. Sust Cities Soc 99:104985"); Wang and Su, [2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR74 "Wang Q, Su M (2019) The effects of urbanization and industrialization on decoupling economic growth from carbon emission – A case study of China. Sust Cities Soc 51:101758")). Cities are predicted to be utilized as major venues for creating issues and answers for huge environmental concerns and global sustainable development, as centers of human activity that directly and indirectly govern biophysical systemic changes (Angelo and Wachsmuth, [2020](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR1 "Angelo H, Wachsmuth D (2020) Why does everyone think cities can save the planet? Urban Stud 57(11):2201–2221"); Camagni et al. [1998](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR10 "Camagni R, Capello R, Nijkamp P (1998) Towards sustainable city policy: an economy-environment technology nexus. Ecol Econ 24(1):103–118"); Elmqvist et al. [2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR21 "Elmqvist T, Andersson E, Frantzeskaki N et al. (2019) Sustainability and resilience for transformation in the urban century. Nat Sustain 2(4):267–273"); Grimm et al. [2008](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR31 "Grimm NB, Faeth SH, Golubiewski NE et al. (2008) Global change and the ecology of cities. Science 319(5864):756–760"); Repette et al. [2021](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR59 "Repette P, Sabatini-Marques J, Yigitcanlar T et al. (2021) The Evolution of City-as-a-Platform: Smart urban development governance with collective knowledge-based platform urbanism. Land 10(33):1–25"); Yang and Zhao, [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR81 "Yang C, Zhao S (2023) Scaling of Chinese urban CO2 emissions and multiple dimensions of city size. Sci Total Environ 857:159502")). These viewpoints are backed up by research from both established large cities and emerging cities experiencing significant expansion (Bai et al. [2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR3 "Bai X, Roberts B, Chen J (2010) Urban sustainability experiments in Asia: patterns and pathways. Environ Sci Policy 13(4):312–325"); Haas, [2012](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR32 "Haas T (2012) Sustainable urbanism and beyond: rethinking cities for the future. Rizzoli, New York"); Newton, [2008](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR53 "Newton PW (2008) Transitions: pathways towards sustainable urban development in Australia. CSIRO Pub, Collingwood, Australia")). The residents and administrations of these cities are confronted with a plethora of issues related to fast transformation. Cities would face shrinkage and unsustainability in the absence of rational planning and management, especially for many industrial cities (Li et al. [2016](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR39 "Li Y, Beeton RJS, Halog A et al. (2016) Evaluating urban sustainability potential based on material flow analysis of inputs and outputs: A case study in Jinchang City, China. Resour Conserv Recycl 110:87–98"); Long and Wu, [2016](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR43 "Long Y, Wu K (2016) Shrinking cities in a rapidly urbanizing China. Environ Plann A 48(2):220–222"); Wu and Wang, [2020](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR80 "Wu K, Wang X (2020) Understanding growth and shrinkage phenomena of industrial and trade cities in Southeastern China: Case study of Yiwu. J Urban Plan Dev 146(4):05020028")). Most of these concerns are associated with the United Nations 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development in achieving the SDG11 of sustainable cities and human settlements (Berisha et al. [2022](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR7 "Berisha E, Caprioli C, Cotella G (2022) Unpacking SDG target 11.a: What is it about and how to measure its progress? City Environ Interact 14:100080"); United Nations, [2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR72 "United Nations (2015) Transforming our world: the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. Sustainable Development Goals: United Nations Sustainable knowledge platform")).

Sustainability research has been transdisciplinary and experienced a shift from protecting the environment and maintaining natural systems to social dimensions involving perception and values, behavioural options, governance, and politics (Loorbach et al. [2017](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR44 "Loorbach D, Frantzeskaki N, Avelino F (2017) Sustainability transitions research: transforming science and practice for societal change. Annu Rev Environ Resour 42(1):599–626"); Wittmayer et al. [2016](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR77 "Wittmayer JM, van Steenbergen F, Rok A et al. (2016) Governing sustainability: a dialogue between Local Agenda 21 and transition management. Local Environ 21(8):939–955")). It emphasises the cross-complementarities across the environmental, economic, and social dimensions, to illustrate complex systems, understand human-environment interactions, respond to realistic needs, and encourage a “revolution” in sustainability (Clark, [2007](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR15 "Clark WC (2007) Sustainability science: A room of its own. P Natl Acad Sci 104(6):1737–1738"); Giddings et al. [2002](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR28 "Giddings B, Hopwood B, O’brien G (2002) Environment, economy and society: fitting them together into sustainable development. Sustain Dev 10(4):187–196"); Kates et al. [2001](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR36 "Kates RW, Clark WC, Corell R et al. (2001) Sustainability Science. Science 292(5517):641–642")). The concept of urban sustainability or sustainable cities has been as an essential subject in seeking global sustainability. Urban sustainability research is developing towards interdisciplinary research integrating the resources, ecology, environment, social and institutional aspects, which enlightens the importance of multiple approaches integration in research and practical world (Li et al. [2020](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR38 "Li G, Kou C, Wang Y et al. (2020) System dynamics modelling for improving urban resilience in Beijing, China. Resour Conserv Recycl 161:104954"); Ribeiro and Gonçalves, [2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR60 "Ribeiro PJG, Gonçalves LAPJ (2019) Urban resilience: A conceptual framework. Sust Cities Soc 50:101625"); Spiekermann and Wegener, [2003](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR67 "Spiekermann K, Wegener M (2003) Modelling urban sustainability. Int J Urban Sci 7(1):47–64")). Transitions toward urban sustainability need to address the key issues framed in social and ethical considerations, technical capability, political and cultural settings, and knowledge-to-action linkages (Peter, [2021](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR56 "Peter C (2021) Social innovation for sustainable urban developmental transitions in Sub-Saharan Africa: Leveraging economic ecosystems and the entrepreneurial state. Sustainability 13(13):7360"); Pira, [2021](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR57 "Pira M (2021) A novel taxonomy of smart sustainable city indicators. Hum Soc Sci Commun 8(1):197"); Weinstein, [2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR76 "Weinstein MP (2010) Sustainability science: the emerging paradigm and the ecology of cities. Sustain-Sci Pr Pol 6(1):1–5")). It was acknowledged that the sociocultural environment has transcended the influence of the natural environment on urban development to dominate the interaction between people and urban systems. This implies that combining scientific research with governance aids in achieving sustainability objectives (Beeton and Lynch, [2012](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR6 "Beeton RJS, Lynch AJJ (2012) Most of nature: A framework to resolve the twin dilemmas of the decline of nature and rural communities. Environ Sci Policy 23:45–56"); Chan and Siu, [2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR12 "Chan KS, Siu YFP (2015) Urban governance and social sustainability: Effects of urban renewal policies in Hong Kong and Macao. Asian Educ Dev Stud 4(3):330–342"); Smedby and Neij, [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR65 "Smedby N, Neij L (2013) Experiences in urban governance for sustainability: The Constructive Dialogue in Swedish municipalities. J Clean Prod 50:148–158")).

Understanding how the urban systems work plays an essential role in promoting transitions toward sustainability. While a variety of methods and strategies have been employed to the understanding of urban systems and to support multifaceted urban transition initiatives, from conceptual framework building to urban systems modelling, spatial-temporal analysis, sustainability evaluation, transition governance et al., in context of diverse social-cultural-institutional background and perspectives (Chang et al. [2020](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR13 "Chang NB, Hossain U, Valencia A et al. (2020) The role of food-energy-water nexus analyses in urban growth models for urban sustainability: A review of synergistic framework. Sust Cities Soc 63:102486"); Mutisya and Yarime, [2014](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR49 "Mutisya E, Yarime M (2014) Moving towards urban sustainability in Kenya: a framework for integration of environmental, economic, social and governance dimensions. Sustain Sci 9(2):205–215"); Nevens et al. [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR52 "Nevens F, Frantzeskaki N, Gorissen L et al. (2013) Urban transition labs: co-creating transformative action for sustainable cities. J Clean Prod 50:111–122"); Turcu, [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR71 "Turcu C (2013) Re-thinking sustainability indicators: local perspectives of urban sustainability. J Environ Plan Manag 56(5):695–719"); Wamsler, [2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR73 "Wamsler C (2015) Mainstreaming ecosystem-based adaptation: Transformation toward sustainability in urban governance and planning. Ecol Soc 20(2):30"); Yin et al. [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR82 "Yin H, Xiao R, Fei X et al. (2023) Analyzing “economy-society-environment” sustainability from the perspective of urban spatial structure: A case study of the Yangtze River delta urban agglomeration. Sust Cities Soc 96:104691")). Among the numerous studies, the urban metabolism models are widely adopted as approaches to simulate the operation of urban systems and find solutions to sustainable issues. The notion of urban metabolism views the city as an ecosystem where materials and energy circulate, are converted and used in different ways, and are subsequently released as waste, valuable products, or pollutants into the environment (Wolman, [1965](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR78 "Wolman A (1965) The metabolism of cities. Sci Am 213(3):179–190"); Girardet, [2014](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR29 "Girardet H (2014) The Metabolism of Cities” from Creating Sustainable Cities (1999). In: Wheeler SM, Beatley T (Eds.) The Sustainable Urban Development Reader, 3rd ed. Routledge, London and New York, p 197–204"); Goldstein et al. [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR30 "Goldstein B, Birkved M, Quitzau MB et al. (2013) Quantification of urban metabolism through coupling with the life cycle assessment framework: concept development and case study. Environ Res Lett 8(3):1–14"); Zhang, [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR84 "Zhang Y (2023) Urban Metabolism: Theory, Methods and Applications. Springer Nature")). A highly efficient metabolism enables high rates of material reuse and recycling as a practical means of enhancing urban sustainability.

In addition to being a physical issue, urban sustainability has become more socially relevant, as cities are self-organizing entities that integrate the interrelated components of material distribution and social networks as they grow as coupled human and natural systems (Liu et al. [2007](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR41 "Liu J, Dietz T, Carpenter SR et al. (2007) Complexity of coupled human and natural systems. Science 317(5844):1513–1516. 36(8):639–649"); Wong, [2021](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR79 "Wong ME (2021) Revisiting Jane Jacobs’s urban complexity in global sustainability city discourse. AMPS 19(1):3")). Sustainable transitions have been a non-linear multilevel dynamic equilibrium transformation process that has a strong societal base (Loorbach et al. [2017](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR44 "Loorbach D, Frantzeskaki N, Avelino F (2017) Sustainability transitions research: transforming science and practice for societal change. Annu Rev Environ Resour 42(1):599–626")). Multidisciplinary efforts have been made to attempt sustainable solutions that incorporate transitions from environmental preservation and resource conservation to social changes within a cultural setting (Hopwood et al. [2005](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR34 "Hopwood B, Mellor M, O’Brien G (2005) Sustainable development: mapping different approaches. Sustain Dev 13(1):38–52"); Todorov and Marinova, [2011](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR69 "Todorov V, Marinova D (2011) Modelling sustainability. Math Comput Simula 81(7):1397–1408")). Many studies have endeavoured to advance urban transitions based on frameworks of sustainability, adaptation, and resilience et al. (Childers et al. [2014](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR14 "Childers DL, Pickett STA, Grove JM et al. (2014) Advancing urban sustainability theory and action: Challenges and opportunities. Landsc Urban Plan 125:320–328"); McCormick et al. [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR46 "McCormick K, Anderberg S, Coenen L et al. (2013) Advancing sustainable urban transformation. J Clean Prod 50:1–11"); Nevens et al. [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR52 "Nevens F, Frantzeskaki N, Gorissen L et al. (2013) Urban transition labs: co-creating transformative action for sustainable cities. J Clean Prod 50:111–122"); Wang et al. [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR75 "Wang Z, Lin L, Zhang B et al. (2023) Sustainable urban development based on an adaptive cycle model: A coupled social and ecological land use development model. Ecol Indic 154:110666")). There are few successful efforts and structural changes that have the potential to dramatically reorient urbanization toward sustainability (Castán Broto et al. [2019](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR11 "Castán Broto V, Trencher G, Iwaszuk E et al. (2019) Transformative capacity and local action for urban sustainability. Ambio 48(5):449–462")). The importance of human actions and initiatives that link with sustainable actions has been emphasised for urban transitions, which calls for bottom-up, participatory, and negotiated approaches (Neuhoff et al. [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR51 "Neuhoff R, Simeone L, Laursen LH (2023) Forms of participatory futuring for urban sustainability: A systematic review. Futures 154:103268"); Turcu, [2013](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR71 "Turcu C (2013) Re-thinking sustainability indicators: local perspectives of urban sustainability. J Environ Plan Manag 56(5):695–719")). Consequently, finding solutions for sustainable urban transitions involves more consideration of governance, social factors, and their practical applications (Chan and Siu, [2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR12 "Chan KS, Siu YFP (2015) Urban governance and social sustainability: Effects of urban renewal policies in Hong Kong and Macao. Asian Educ Dev Stud 4(3):330–342"); Colantonio and Dixon, [2011](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR18 "Colantonio A, Dixon TJ (2011) Urban regeneration & social sustainability: best practice from European cities. Wiley-Blackwell, Oxford"); Ernst et al. [2016](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR22 "Ernst L, de Graaf-Van Dinther RE, Peek GJ et al. (2016) Sustainable urban transformation and sustainability transitions; conceptual framework and case study. J Clean Prod 112:2988–2999"); Sareen and Waagsaether, [2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR63 "Sareen S, Waagsaether KL (2023) New municipalism and the governance of urban transitions to sustainability. Urban Stud 60(11):2271–2289"); Weinstein, [2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-024-03598-x#ref-CR76 "Weinstein MP (2010) Sustainability science: the emerging paradigm and the ecology of cities. Sustain-Sci Pr Pol 6(1):1–5")).

Despite notable transformations in the urban sustainability paradigm, there is a relative paucity of systematic research on sustainability transition that combines knowledge of urban physical and social systems with strategic responses to sustainability concerns. Especially less attention has been paid to the effects attributed to social elements and human responses, which are critical to the practical dimensions of sustainable transitions. These concerns bring a sense of urgency to understand the complex urban system, by answering questions of how the urban biophysical system works, how humans act in the urban social system, and how to facilitate urban sustainability transitions in regional resource-environmental and socio-economic status quo. Abstract
--------

Responding to calls for climate action, this article develops and introduces a Sustainability Transition Framework (STF), which is a high-level interpretation of the complex dynamics dominating the current historical moment characterised by an aspiration for accelerated climate action and challenges with implementing change. This STF brings together and integrates a set of theories about change which were developed in different disciplines and not commonly discussed at the same time. The four key theories are: (1) sustainability transitions, transition management and related theories which consider the necessary change in sociotechnical regimes; (2) diffusion of innovation theory, which provides a high-level overview of patterns with uptake of innovation; (3) a change curve which explains complex emotional experiences when facing organisational change; and (4) a conceptualisation of the style cycle, which is commonly used in the history of art and architecture. These are visually depicted and integrated into a novel STF diagram. The STF presents one of the most comprehensive integrations of currently available knowledge on how change happens, which is essential for accelerated climate action. The STF helps understand individual or social group experiences. Through an improved understanding of the sustainability transition, the STF empowers climate action.

1. Introduction
---------------

We live in a complex historical moment where the science is unequivocally saying that human activities since 1850 have caused an increase in global surface temperature, and that this trend has continued to increase in the last decade, 2010–2019 [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B1-sustainability-16-00217)]. Numerous news reports appear to agree that the change in climate is starting to be felt across the globe, and even climate migration is on the rise [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B2-sustainability-16-00217)]. However, research shows that people respond to climate change facts through a range of individual lenses, including personality traits, values, political views, etc., but also that our individual responses are mediated by a range of assumptions about what is normal behaviour compared to others [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B3-sustainability-16-00217),[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B4-sustainability-16-00217),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B5-sustainability-16-00217),[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B6-sustainability-16-00217)]. As a consequence, even direct personal experience of extreme weather events does not intensely correlate to a heightened recognition of climate change [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B3-sustainability-16-00217)]. At the same time, climate anxiety is increasingly recognised as an issue and discussed, especially among the younger generations [[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B7-sustainability-16-00217),[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B8-sustainability-16-00217)]. 

Arguably, for architecture and related disciplines, these discussions are even more complex. In order to meet the 2030 and 2050 goals set to limit global warming to 1.5–2 °C, buildings and the built environment must contribute a significant reduction in CO 2 emissions [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B1-sustainability-16-00217)]. Currently, it remains unclear how this can be achieved, given that, despite much effort, emissions from the construction industry globally are still increasing [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B1-sustainability-16-00217)]. Further to that, architecture is one of the areas where energy conservation and sustainability started to be discussed in the early 1970s [[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B9-sustainability-16-00217)], which begs the question: where is the progress one would expect after half a century of effort? Complicating things further, many schools of architecture had included courses on sustainability by around 2000, but some sustainable practices appear to have gone backwards [[10](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B10-sustainability-16-00217)]. Indeed, since around 2000, the global consumption of materials for construction has exponentially increased [[11](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B11-sustainability-16-00217),[12](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B12-sustainability-16-00217)].

Jointly, this global sense of urgency, the disciplinary agreement that architecture should be part of the solution, and the lack of obvious success can lead to a sense of confusion, discouragement, and delays at the time when acceleration is very much needed. 

Responding to this context, this article introduces a Sustainability Transition Framework (STF). The aim of the STF is to develop an interpretation of the existing complexities to offer a sense of clarity as to why the current moment is complex, and to use this clarity to empower action. The STF provides a simple, high-level view of the transitions generally. The core proposition of this article is that understanding the patterns of the change process can help accelerate action, because it aids sensemaking. The STF enables us to articulate an overall view of the changes around us, which can give us confidence in our own power to make a positive difference and clarity on what needs doing, and therefore can be very helpful during these unsettled times. 

#### Introducing the Sustainability Transition Framework (STF)

The Sustainability Transition Framework (STF) brings together a set of theories about change which were developed in different disciplines and are not commonly discussed at the same time. Some of these theories are fairly recent, while others are much older. The four key existing conceptualisations of change, discussed in [Section 3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#sec3-sustainability-16-00217), are: (1) sustainability transitions, transition management and related theories as one set of conceptualisations considering the necessary sociotechnical regime change to achieve sustainable outcomes; (2) diffusion of innovation theory, which provides a high-level overview of patterns with uptake of innovation; (3) a change curve which is based on the five stages of grieving and explains the complex emotions experienced when facing organisational change; and (4) a conceptualisation of the style cycle, which is common in the history of art and architecture. Each of these theories explain one aspect of the change process; by considering the dynamics through the important regime shift that is needed (1); the adoption of innovative solutions (2); the emotional complexities experienced by all of us (3); and by attempting to articulate how these aspects relate to the change of style in architecture and other design disciplines (4). However, this article asserts that these theories are most effective when considered jointly, and that these can be effectively brought together. 

Although some aspects of the framework are contextualised within architecture and the built environment, overall, this framework applies to any situation which is facing any complex transitions. In fact, the STF presents one of the most comprehensive integrations of currently available knowledge on how change happens, which is essential for climate action in any area, and therefore possibly contributes more to the understanding of sustainability transitions generally than specifically to sustainability transitions in architecture and the built environment. 

In addition to considering this set of theories at the same time, the article presents their visualisations, and visually brings these together into an integrated STF diagram. The resulting diagram achieves a high level of simplicity when describing very complex patterns. Of course, that requires simplifications. In fact, even the four key theories which are brought together into the STF have already received criticisms for their relative tendency towards simplification. 

Simplicity helps clarity because simplicity, rather than complexity, is psychologically desirable. A bias towards simplicity and abstraction has a long history of study in psychology and its application in relation to learning [[13](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B13-sustainability-16-00217),[14](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B14-sustainability-16-00217),[15](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B15-sustainability-16-00217)]. Although some aspects of this bias could still be explained more fully, it is already established that the human ‘mind seeks the simplest available interpretation of observations’ [[14](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B14-sustainability-16-00217)]. This appears to be true for a range of situations, including perception (finding tasks), memory (learning), cognition (problem solving tasks, rational analysis, etc.), and is equally applicable in nature, due to the Universal Law of Generalisation [[13](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B13-sustainability-16-00217),[14](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B14-sustainability-16-00217),[15](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B15-sustainability-16-00217)]. This could be why most of the theories discussed here are still in use (key books still in print), some more than half a century since their first release.

However, whilst accepting and proactively working with this simplicity bias, it is also critical to acknowledge that very few phenomena can be fully explained through a simple summary. Rather, the simple, high-level summary can be seen as a table of contents enabling the outsider of the field to grasp it somewhat, while deciding how to dive deeper into it. This helps the sensemaking, and sensemaking has been recognised as important in helping alter recipients’ understanding and support the process of change [[16](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B16-sustainability-16-00217),[17](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B17-sustainability-16-00217),[18](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/1/217#B18-sustainability-16-00217)]. Abstract
--------

Significant global sustainability challenges include among others, energy, climate, and sanitation. Previous Sustainability Transition research has attempted to understand transformation complexity and interdependence, primarily through single-case methodological studies or large-scale analytical frameworks such as the Multi-Level Perspective. This leaves a knowledge gap on common dynamics underlying transition processes and emergent behaviors. To fill this gap, we conducted a cross-sectoral analysis of five system dynamics sustainability transition models with the objective of finding a common system archetype. An archetype emerged from a multi-step, mixed method structural analysis of these models. The extracted archetype captures generalizable sustainable transition dynamics across a diversity of research domains and temporal scales. The structural drivers of sustainability transitions within this archetype are used to discuss future research and practice that seeks to provide insight on common transition dynamics, deeper clarity on leverage points capable of managing transitions, and a framework for subsequent transition modeling archetype analyses.

Introduction
------------

Solving humanity's grand challenges such as climate change (IPCC,2018), decarbonizing energy consumption (Sovacool,2016), or inadequate access to sanitation and clean water (Walters and Javernick-Will,2015; Prouty et al., 2020) requires restructuring the socio-technical systems (STS) providing society with the needed services. Each STS is composed of a highly interdependent hierarchy of actors, firms or organizations, operational practices or standards, accumulated knowledge, cultural norms and regulatory agencies interactively providing the sector's service to society (Markard et al., 2012; Geels,2004). Solving such challenges transcends simple technology replacement; a widespread technological shift also fundamentally transforms the STS, restructuring the actors, firms or organizations involved, with subsequent impacts on practices, knowledge, cultural norms, and regulations. Such a change may be defined as a sustainability transition (ST), where a large-scale reordering of technologies, market dynamics, user preferences and social perceptions (Edmondson et al., 2019) result in new types of STS (Elzen et al., 2004; Grin et al., 2010). Transition research has expanded in the last decade to encompass studies in a variety of sectors, geographies, and methodological approaches. A common theme found in these studies is the need for multidimensional perspectives capable of incorporating the feedback-rich, complex nature of transitions (Geels,2004, 2002; Köhler et al., 2019).

One transition research perspective focuses on the macro level. These ‘top-down’ analytical frameworks and methodologies have employed large scale historical studies (Markard et al., 2012; Köhler et al., 2019), including the Multi-Level Perspective (Geels,2002), Strategic Niche Management (Kemp et al., 1998) and Transition Management (Loorbach,2010; Rotmans et al., 2016). Others have used meta-analyses (Wiseman et al., 2013), transition pathway typologies (Geels and Schot,2007), and innovation systems studies (Hekkert and Negro,2009) to better understand transition complexity. The other common approach to transition studies is ‘bottom-up’, focusing on in-depth, single-case studies conducted at regional to supra-national scales (Köhler et al., 2019). The difference in scale between these two approaches has left a knowledge gap regarding the underlying processes and emergent behavior of transition dynamics. Research exploring the gap between macro-scale and single case studies may increase clarity on transition dynamics but requires balancing complexity with a greater degree of abstraction (Köhler et al., 2019). One promising methodology capable of bridging case-specific descriptive studies with macro-level perspectives is modeling, whether qualitative, quantitative or both (Köhler et al., 2019; Li et al., 2015). Modeling can simplify ST system complexity, provide clarity on attributes and the dynamics between them, as well as allow for systematic testing of interventions (Holtz et al., 2015). Policy makers have traditionally relied on quantitative linear or nonlinear programming, general equilibrium (Gottschamer and Zhang,2016) or Integrative Assessment Models (IAM's) methods to better understand interlinked sectors such as energy, water, climate, and agriculture (Trutnevyte et al., 2019; Bolwig et al., 2019). These types of quantitative models are capable of simulating scenarios to better understand future behavior of non-linear socio-technical systems (Hirt et al., 2020; Moallemi et al., 2017). However, these methods are not fully suited to capture dynamic feedbacks found in sustainable, socio-technical transitions (Gottschamer and Zhang,2020; Li et al., 2015).

This study integrates three separate, but complementary transition research domains to identify mid-level structural patterns (archetypes) in sustainability transitions; System Dynamics (SD) modeling, Socio-Ecological Archetype analysis, and a broadly used analytical framework used to examine transitions called The Multilevel Perspective (MLP). SD is a suitable methodology for studying non-linearities and emergent properties found in transitions (Köhler et al., 2018; Köhler et al., 2019; Papachristos,2019) and has been extensively applied in transition research. The breadth of SD transition research has resulted in a significant number of models available for use as case studies in a socio-ecological archetype analysis. This integrated systems approach employed sustainability transition case studies as a unit of analysis to identify recurring structures and patterns shaping the sustainability of socio-ecological systems (Oberlack et al., 2019; Piemontese et al., 2022). The archetype emerging from the case study analysis was then situated within the MLP literature, highlighting its contribution to both the MLP and sustainable transitions discourse. To our knowledge, no such archetype analysis of extant SD models exists. The SD methodology was selected not only for its ability to model complex socio-technical systems (Papachristos and Struben,2019), but also for its systems thinking perspective. In this perspective, a basic tenet is that a system's behavior (whether desirable or undesirable) is driven by its structure. A system's structure is composed of elements, attributes or factors and the interactions, feedbacks and time delays that exist between them (Meadows,2009). Interestingly, many complex systems have structural patterns and feedbacks that create similar behavior, even across completely different contexts (Meadows,2009). The field of system thinking has identified a set of “system structures that produce common patterns of problematic behavior” called archetypes (Meadows,2009). Please see Senge(1990), Kim (1992, 1994), Braun(2002), and Meadows(2009), for an in-depth discussion of archetypes. One well-known archetype is the “Tragedy of the Commons”, wherein overconsumption of a common resource diminishes its utility for all users (Braun,2002). Specific examples are the degradation of a common pasture used for grazing herd animals, or the rising atmospheric CO2 level due to fossil fuel combustion. Archetypes bridge the gap between complexity and abstraction by depicting generalized structure and behavioral patterns (Senge,1990; Wolstenholme 2003; Wolstenholme,2004). As such, they make useful tools for examining current undesirable behaviors (Braun,2002), provide a deeper understanding of why a behavior manifests (Kim,1994), and can serve as a guide for system interventions, wherein policies or protocols can be evaluated against the drivers of current behavior (Braun,2002). Archetypes can be used to both identify high-leverage intervention points – places in the system where the most beneficial change can be implemented for the least amount of effort (Kim,1992) – and serve as a guide for developing more refined models (Braun,2002).

Recent research has suggested that identifying structural patterns or archetypes found in sustainable transitions may provide insights on how such transitions unfold over time (Please refer to Eisenack et al.2021, Oberlack et al.2019, Sietz et al.2019, Ecology & Society,2021 for an excellent introduction to sustainability archetypes). Abstracting transition complexity in order to extract generalizable transition dynamics is critical to address the knowledge gap between top-down analytical frameworks and bottom-up case-specific analyses. To develop a ST archetype, we conducted a novel cross-sectoral archetype analysis of SD model structures (or interactions between model components) across transportation, energy, biofuel, sanitation, and agricultural sectors. Structural analysis entailed a combined analysis of network centrality and dynamic feedback between key ST attributes modeled within each case study. Examining model structures across a diversity of cases balanced complexity with a greater degree of abstraction. This allows for comparing and contrasting systems leverage points, revealing synergies and tradeoffs for different policies and practices to target multiple transition domains. Identifying a ‘ST’ archetype can lead to greater clarity on why problem behavior manifests, guide the development of future models, and identify system leverage points. This analysis had two main objectives; the first was to identify a framework for moving from case-specific SD modeling to higher order processes and behaviors driving transition dynamics. The second was to identify a common ST archetype capable of informing future modeling and transition research. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. Section 2 provides a succinct review of the MLP and a more in-depth discussion of both its synergies and limitations with SD modeling. Section 3 reviews SD modeling, then provides a discussion on the benefits (and limitations) of an archetype to the transitions discourse. Section 4 details the archetype analysis, while Section 5 presents the methods and lays out the framework for studying the selected models. Section 6 discusses the results and Section 7 contains implications and future work, Section 8 is the limitations and the last is a brief conclusion.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Sustainability transitions encompass complex interactions among environmental, social, and economic factors, requiring interdisciplinary approaches to address diverse challenges and promote holistic solutions.
Frameworks like the Multi-Level Perspective and Sustainability Transition Framework integrate various theories, offering structured approaches to analyze and guide sustainability transitions.

enriched_points.point_title: Need for Ongoing Research and Adaptation
enriched_points.expanded_content: Continuous research is essential to adapt strategies in sustainability transitions, addressing emerging challenges and refining methodologies to enhance effectiveness and resilience.
